jokes.text: They're nothing special. The extra ingredient is care.
jokes.setup: Homer compliments Marge's pork chops as the best in the world
jokes.punchline: Marge deflects with wholesome sincerity about 'care' being the secret ingredient
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge earnestness that's endearing rather than hilarious. Well-integrated character moment.

jokes.text: A sprinkle of chervil, half a teaspoon of turmeric and a whisper of MSG.
jokes.setup: Marge listing fancy spices in a poetic manner
jokes.punchline: Ends the fancy ingredient list with pedestrian MSG
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from fancy herbs to MSG, with nice poetic language ('whisper of MSG'). Economy and structure work well.

jokes.text: Volume One: Spice Racks.
jokes.setup: Homer consulting 'The Complete Handyman's Bookshelf' for building a spice rack
jokes.punchline: The entire bookshelf is just Volume One about spice racks
jokes.explanation: Absurd specificity that's economical and unexpected. Good setup-payoff structure.

jokes.text: Pick up a hammer.
jokes.setup: Homer reading instructions from the spice rack manual
jokes.punchline: The first instruction is overly basic - just picking up a hammer
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - the absurd over-simplification of instructions works with Homer's incompetence.

jokes.text: Not as easy as I thought it was.
jokes.setup: Homer attempts to pick up a hammer per the instructions
jokes.punchline: He struggles with this most basic task
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his incompetence at the most basic level is well-established and funny.

jokes.text: Dad, can you move your head? / I can't. It's broken.
jokes.setup: Homer apparently injured himself building the spice rack
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact delivery about his head being 'broken'
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy payoff to the spice rack disaster, with Homer's typical understated response.

jokes.text: Where would a child get the idea to attack her father with a mallet?
jokes.setup: Maggie has apparently attacked Homer with a mallet
jokes.punchline: Marge asks rhetorically where she could have gotten such an idea (while watching violent cartoons)
jokes.explanation: Sets up the episode's central premise with good ironic timing - the answer is obviously the violent cartoons.

jokes.text: But if you take them away, we'll grow up without humor and be robots. / Really? What kind of robots?
jokes.setup: Bart argues they need cartoons or they'll become robots
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely missing the point and being interested in robot specifics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - completely misses the argument and gets distracted by irrelevant details. Perfect character comedy with great economy.

jokes.text: Hey, that's just crazy enough to work.
jokes.setup: Nelson suggesting watching cartoons at his house
jokes.punchline: Bart treating this obvious solution as brilliant strategy
jokes.explanation: Familiar joke structure - treating obvious solution as ingenious plan

jokes.text: I told you. My baby beat me up.
jokes.setup: Homer calling in to work
jokes.punchline: His excuse for not coming in is that his baby beat him up
jokes.explanation: Absurd but delivered matter-of-factly. The humiliation of being beaten up by a baby is inherently funny.

jokes.text: It is not the worst excuse I ever thought up. Wise guy.
jokes.setup: Homer defending his excuse about the baby
jokes.punchline: He admits to having worse excuses, then gets defensive
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Homer's history of bad excuses and defensiveness.

jokes.text: Because why? / I said so. / You said so why? / I'm trying to work. / What are you doing? / Cataloguing the violence.
jokes.setup: Typical parent-child 'why' chain about watching cartoons
jokes.punchline: Homer is 'working' by cataloguing cartoon violence
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with the reveal that Homer's 'work' is just watching cartoons.

jokes.text: I don't think adults have ever sat down and watched them before.
jokes.setup: Marge watching violent cartoons with adult eyes
jokes.punchline: The observation that adults don't usually watch kids' shows critically
jokes.explanation: Meta commentary on the episode's premise - adults examining children's entertainment.

jokes.text: What kind of warped human being would find that funny?
jokes.setup: Marge watching cartoon violence
jokes.punchline: She's describing the show's own audience who laugh at Itchy & Scratchy
jokes.explanation: Meta joke that implicates the audience - we're the 'warped' people who find cartoon violence funny.

jokes.text: What kind of warped human being would find that funny?
jokes.setup: Marge watching violent cartoons while cataloguing violence
jokes.punchline: Her moral outrage delivered while the audience presumably finds the cartoons funny
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on cartoon violence but delivered fairly straightforwardly without much comedic sophistication.

jokes.text: As to your comments, research shows one person can't make a difference... no matter how screwy she is.
jokes.setup: Corporate response letter to Marge's complaint
jokes.punchline: Dismissive and insulting while maintaining corporate politeness
jokes.explanation: Good satire of corporate dismissiveness disguised as professionalism.

jokes.text: I'll show them what one screwball can do!
jokes.setup: Marge reading the dismissive letter
jokes.punchline: She embraces the 'screwball' label and declares war
jokes.explanation: Good character moment where Marge reclaims the insult and gets motivated.

jokes.text: How long are we gonna do this? / I've never changed the world. I don't know how long it takes.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about their protest duration
jokes.punchline: Marge's logical but naive response about world-changing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge earnestness - she's approaching world-changing like a recipe she's never tried.

jokes.text: What is 'S-N-U-H'? / Snuh. It's 'Springfieldians for Nonviolence, Understanding and Helping.'
jokes.setup: Ned reading Marge's protest sign acronym
jokes.punchline: The acronym spells 'SNUH' and is pronounced as such
jokes.explanation: Good acronym joke - the awkward pronunciation and forced organization name work well.

jokes.text: I didn't know they made TV dinners this bad.
jokes.setup: Family eating terrible food while Marge is distracted by her crusade
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact observation about the food quality
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor about the consequences of Marge's distraction.

jokes.text: There's peas in my cobbler. / They're everywhere.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about peas in dessert
jokes.punchline: Marge's distracted response suggests peas are in everything
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the food disaster with Marge's distracted response.

jokes.text: Twenty million women, and I had to marry Jane Fonda.
jokes.setup: Marge becoming a political activist
jokes.punchline: Homer compares her to Jane Fonda, the archetypal activist
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference that captures Homer's perspective on activist wives.

jokes.text: Hey! Settle down, boys and girls. Or Krusty will bring out his friend Corporal Punishment again.
jokes.setup: Kids are booing and being disruptive
jokes.punchline: Krusty threatens them with 'Corporal Punishment' (both military rank and physical discipline)
jokes.explanation: Excellent pun that works on multiple levels - military character name that's also a threat.

jokes.text: Drop an anvil? / Hit her with a piano? / Stuff her with TNT, throw a match and run?
jokes.setup: Executives asking how to stop Marge
jokes.punchline: Writers suggest cartoon violence solutions to their cartoon violence problem
jokes.explanation: Brilliant irony - they want to solve their violence problem with more violence, showing they don't understand the criticism.

jokes.text: All your degrees and that's the best you can do? You make me sick.
jokes.setup: Writers suggesting cartoon violence as solutions
jokes.punchline: Myers disgusted by their stupidity while missing his own hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing executive arrogance and lack of self-awareness.

jokes.text: I'm so funny!
jokes.setup: Child watching the new non-violent Itchy & Scratchy
jokes.punchline: Desperate attempt to find humor in the bland content
jokes.explanation: Captures the awkward forced enthusiasm when entertainment becomes bland.

jokes.text: Some of these are good. I never knew mice lived such lives.
jokes.setup: Homer reading fan mail about the new cartoons
jokes.punchline: He takes the cartoon literally, thinking real mice live like this
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer literalism - he doesn't understand the concept of cartoon fantasy.

jokes.text: Are cartoons too violent? Most people say, 'No! What a stupid question!'
jokes.setup: News show introducing the cartoon violence debate
jokes.punchline: Dismissive framing that shows media bias before the debate starts
jokes.explanation: Good satire of media bias and leading questions in supposedly balanced coverage.

jokes.text: There was the crusades. Tremendous violence! Many killed!
jokes.setup: Myers arguing that violence existed before cartoons
jokes.punchline: Oversimplified, caveman-like description of the Crusades
jokes.explanation: Good parody of oversimplified historical arguments with primitive language.

jokes.text: It went on for 30 years. / This was before cartoons?
jokes.setup: Myers explaining the Crusades predated cartoons
jokes.punchline: Kent's incredulous confirmation as if this is shocking news
jokes.explanation: Kent's fake amazement at obvious historical fact satirizes TV host reactions.

jokes.text: I'm referring to women who love too much, fear of winning, sex-aholism.
jokes.setup: Dr. Monroe discussing real psychological problems
jokes.punchline: Made-up condition 'sex-aholism' mixed with real psychology terms
jokes.explanation: Parodies pop psychology with made-up conditions that sound plausible.

jokes.text: One of my guilty little pleasures is to turn on Itchy & Scratchy... and laugh myself silly.
jokes.setup: Psychologist being asked about cartoon violence
jokes.punchline: He admits to enjoying the violence himself as a 'guilty pleasure'
jokes.explanation: Shows hypocrisy of expert who enjoys what he should professionally condemn.

jokes.text: I won't watch your show, buy your products... or brake if I see you crossing the street.
jokes.setup: Angry viewer letter about cartoon violence
jokes.punchline: Escalates from boycott to actual vehicular threat
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from passive boycott to active threat. Perfect structure building to the punchline.

jokes.text: Make it a pie. Easier to draw.
jokes.setup: Writers trying to figure out non-violent cartoon ending
jokes.punchline: Animation decision based on drawing convenience, not story
jokes.explanation: Good meta joke about animation production priorities - convenience over storytelling.

jokes.text: Couldn't Itchy share his pie with Scratchy? Then they'd both have pie.
jokes.setup: Marge asked to solve the cartoon's conflict
jokes.punchline: Her wholesome, overly simple solution to cartoon conflict
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - her maternal instinct to solve conflicts with sharing.

jokes.text: It's different, I'll give you that.
jokes.setup: Marge's sharing solution to cartoon violence
jokes.punchline: Myers' diplomatic but unenthusiastic response
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing executive's polite horror at the bland solution.

jokes.text: Itchy and Scratchy have lost their edge. / It's a nice message of sharing. / I think it sucks.
jokes.setup: Kids reacting to the new non-violent cartoon
jokes.punchline: Baby Maggie has the harshest critique
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with Maggie delivering the most brutal review. Perfect character progression.

jokes.text: We went fishing. Almost caught a catfish this big. / I went bird watching and saw a grackle.
jokes.setup: Kids describing their day without cartoons
jokes.punchline: They're doing wholesome activities but sound bored
jokes.explanation: Good contrast showing kids doing 'healthy' activities but lacking their usual energy.

jokes.text: Wow! What great kids. This is the golden age, Marge!
jokes.setup: Kids being unusually calm and wholesome
jokes.punchline: Homer's oblivious enthusiasm for the boring result
jokes.explanation: Homer missing that the kids are actually less interesting now - his simple priorities.

jokes.text: Filth. It portrays parts of the body which, though practical, are evil.
jokes.setup: Helen condemning Michelangelo's David
jokes.punchline: Describing body parts as 'practical' but 'evil'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic - acknowledging anatomy is practical while calling it evil.

jokes.text: I like that statue. / I knew she was soft on nudity.
jokes.setup: Marge defending the David statue
jokes.punchline: Helen treating art appreciation as moral failing
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Helen's extremism and Marge's isolation.

jokes.text: A masterpiece? Or just a guy with his pants down?
jokes.setup: Introducing debate about Michelangelo's David
jokes.punchline: Reducing classical art to crude description
jokes.explanation: Good reductive description that captures how art looks to uncultured viewers.

jokes.text: We want your big Italian butt out of here!
jokes.setup: Protesters confronting the David statue
jokes.punchline: The crude, direct insult to a classical art masterpiece
jokes.explanation: Decent culture clash humor but relies on crude language for impact rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: Aren't you Marge Simpson, the wacko?
jokes.setup: Kent interviewing Marge again
jokes.punchline: Casually calling her 'the wacko' on live TV
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier dismissive labels, showing how media frames activists.

jokes.text: One person can make a difference but probably shouldn't.
jokes.setup: Marge reflecting on her crusade's consequences
jokes.punchline: Philosophical conclusion that individual action might be misguided
jokes.explanation: Excellent philosophical punchline that subverts typical activist messages. Very quotable and thoughtful.

jokes.text: Tomorrow our topic will be 'Religion: Which Is the One True Faith?'
jokes.setup: Kent closing the show
jokes.punchline: Next topic is impossibly controversial and presumptuous
jokes.explanation: Good satire of TV shows tackling impossible questions as if they're solvable debates.

jokes.text: There he is, Michelangelo's Dave. / David.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to show cultural appreciation
jokes.punchline: Gets the name wrong, calling David 'Dave'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - trying to be cultured but getting basic facts wrong. Very economical.

jokes.text: Really? Why? / They're forcing them!
jokes.setup: Homer saying kids will see the statue
jokes.punchline: His enthusiasm for mandatory art appreciation
jokes.explanation: Homer missing the irony that forced art education isn't ideal either.
